coredata.dc:title: Development of luminescence carbon quantum dots for metal ions detection and photocatalytic degradation of organic dyes from aqueous media
coredata.dc:creator: Alshammari, Ghedeir Muslem
Al-Ayed, Mohammed Suliman
Abdelhalim, Mohamed Anwar
Al-Harbi, Laila Naif
Qasem, Akram Ahmed
Abdo Yahya, Mohammed
coredata.dc:description: In the present study, fish scale waste was used for the organic synthesis of luminescence CQDs by the hydrothermal method. The impact of CQDs on improved photocatalytic degradation of organic dyes and metal ions detection is examined in this study. The synthesized CQDs had a variety of characteristics that were detected, such as crystallinity, morphology, functional groups, and binding energies. The luminescence CQDs showed outstanding photocatalytic effectiveness for the destruction of methylene blue (96.5%) and reactive red 120 dye (97.8%), respectively after 120 min exposure to visible light (420 nm). The high electron transport properties of the CQDs edges, which make it possible to efficiently separate electron-hole pairs, are attributed to the enhanced photocatalytic activity of the CQDs. These degradation results prove that the CQDs are the outcome of a synergistic interaction between visible light (adsorption); a potential mechanism is also suggested, and the kinetics is analyzed to use a pseudo-first-order model. Additionally, the metal ions detection of CQDs was studied by various metal ions (Hg2+, Fe2+, Cu2+, Ni2+, and Cd2+) in an aqueous solution and results revealed that the PL intensity of CQDs in presence of cadmium ions decreased. Studies show that the organic fabrication of CQDs are effective photocatalyst and may one day serve as the ideal material to reduce water pollution.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Luminescence CQDs
Fish-scale waste
Hydrothermal production
Photocatalytic activity
Cd2+ ions detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of CQDs from fish scales
Photocatalytic activity of methylene blue (MB) and reactive red 120 (RR120)
Photocatalytic performance of MB and RR120
Kinetics study of MB and RR120
Stability study of MB and RR120

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns of fish scale wastes synthesized CQDs.
FTIR analysis spectrum of fish scale wastes synthesized CQDs.
HR-TEM images of carbon quantum dots fabricated from fish scale wastes.
XPS analysis of (a) survey scan, (b) N1s, (c) C1s, and (d) O1s of bio-fabrication of luminescence CQDs.
(a) Methylene blue and (b) Reactive Red 120 dyes were subjected to photodegradation rate, (c) degradation efficiency, and (d) mechanism of as-prepared CQDs.
Kinetic study of (a) Methylene blue and (b) Reactive Red 120 dye efficiency for the blank, CQDs-Dark, and CQDs-visible light.
Cycle curves of photocatalytic degradation of (a) methylene blue and (b) reactive red 120 dye solution with the CQDs for 3 runs of reuse.
(a) Metal ion detection, (b) various metal ions, (c) different concentrations of Cd2+ ions (50 μM–250 μM), and (d) linear plot for fluorescence restoration by Cd2+ ions.

head.ce:title: Development of luminescence carbon quantum dots for metal ions detection and photocatalytic degradation of organic dyes from aqueous media

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Food Science & Nutrition, College of Food and Agricultural Sciences, King Saud University, Riyadh, 11451, Saudi Arabia

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Food and Agricultural Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Food Science & Nutrition, College of Food and Agricultural Sciences, King Saud University, Riyadh 11451, Saudi Arabia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics and Astronomy, College of Sciences, King Saud University, Riyadh, 11451, Saudi Arabia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics and Astronomy, College of Sciences, King Saud University, Riyadh 11451, Saudi Arabia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In the present study, fish scale waste was used for the organic synthesis of luminescence CQDs by the hydrothermal method. The impact of CQDs on improved photocatalytic degradation of organic dyes and metal ions detection is examined in this study. The synthesized CQDs had a variety of characteristics that were detected, such as crystallinity, morphology, functional groups, and binding energies. The luminescence CQDs showed outstanding photocatalytic effectiveness for the destruction of methylene blue (96.5%) and reactive red 120 dye (97.8%), respectively after 120 min exposure to visible light (420 nm). The high electron transport properties of the CQDs edges, which make it possible to efficiently separate electron-hole pairs, are attributed to the enhanced photocatalytic activity of the CQDs. These degradation results prove that the CQDs are the outcome of a synergistic interaction between visible light (adsorption); a potential mechanism is also suggested, and the kinetics is analyzed to use a pseudo-first-order model. Additionally, the metal ions detection of CQDs was studied by various metal ions (Hg2+, Fe2+, Cu2+, Ni2+, and Cd2+) in an aqueous solution and results revealed that the PL intensity of CQDs in presence of cadmium ions decreased. Studies show that the organic fabrication of CQDs are effective photocatalyst and may one day serve as the ideal material to reduce water pollution.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: The development of the modern sector worldwide poses a major threat to the both environment and human beings. Corresponding to the World Health Organization lots of humans suffer from gastric worms, cancer, diarrhea, schistosomiasis, and trachoma (Manikandan et al., 2022; Dey et al., 2022). The ecosystem is continuously polluted by the key organic dyes that are released from industrial effluents (Devi et al., 2022). Based on current values, the textile, leather, painting, food, and consumer electronics industries create millions of tons of organic dye and inorganic waste annually (Hwang et al., 2023; Maheshwaran et al., 2022). In today's environment, organic dye contaminants, such as orange II, methyl blue, methyl orange, reactive red 120, and rhodamine B have been found in industrial effluent (Manikandan et al., 2023). To report the imminent ecological issues, various methods, including photocatalysis, ion exchange, metal ion detection, precipitation, activated carbon filtration, membrane separation, and adsorption have been utilized to remove organic dyes from contaminant water (Malavika et al., 2021; Adithya et al., 2021). Among these traditional technologies, photocatalysis represents an adaptable and effective method that can degrade organic dyes using nanomaterials (Joshiba et al., 2021). Periovusly, different nanomaterials such as ZnO, TiO2, Ag2O, Fe2O3, CeO2, and ZrO2, were used in the fields of catalysis, sensors, and environmental remediation of wastewater (Govarthanan et al., 2020). However, those kinds of nanomaterials show significant limitations, including their high recombination rates and the challenges of separating and recycling them (Vijayan et al., 2023).

Recently, quantum dots (QDs) have been created as a new class of effective carbon nanomaterial. QDs are a novel group of 0-dimensional nanomaterials having unique optical properties, high chemical stability, outstanding biocompatibility, and non-toxicity (Khaledian et al., 2021). The quantum dots are an inescapable new group of hybrid flexible nanomaterials that comprise a fickle amount of electrons, which participate and separate quantum atoms (Kabel et al., 2021). These advanced QDs can offer a large spectrum of flexible portion proportions, which could develop luminous properties (Hu et al., 2021). These kinds of hybrid QDs nanomaterials have different unique benefits, of having a flexible size, biocompatibility, and maximum brightness, and can develop a bright low when they are illuminated by UV light (Osypiw et al., 2022; Govindasamy et al., 2022).

In recent years, the luminance properties of different QDs have been utilized in several wastewater applications, sensors, and heavy metal ions detection (Gadore et al., 2023). The summary of these environmentally-safe and harmless QDs starts up new prospects for various environmental applications (Mohamed et al., 2021; Zhao et al., 2021). Additionally, vast study efforts have been placed into using QD in photocatalytic activity and metal ions detection applications. Most recently, Elango et al. (2022) fabricated fluorescent CQDs having antibacterial activity properties and utilized them for the detection of heavy metals. Although numerous CQDs with exceptional metal ion detection and photocatalytic activity were developed from various biosources, very less studies are concentrated on applying these CQDs for photocatalytic activity and detection of metal ions (Abd Rani et al., 2020; You et al., 2022). Chemical-based carbon quantum dots properties with valuable physical characteristics were produced by chemical methods but, the cost involved, environmental hazards posed by the synthesis, and the resultant product make the materials unsuitable for utilization (Zhang et al., 2017). However, CQDs were fabricated from several organic biowaste sources, and the CQDs produced from biowaste seek special attention (Zhang et al., 2019). The raw material required for the synthesis, easily available and stable physical properties, and eco-friendly is the advantages obtained from the CQDs produced from the organic biowastes. The high amount of carbon that occurs in organic biowaste has tuned raised interest in the organic production of carbon quantum dots from biowaste sources (Castañeda-Serna et al., 2022; Palanisamy et al., 2022a,b). The photocatalytic degradation of dye and detection of cadmium ions using bio-fabrication CQDs were investigated. Methylene blue and reactive red dyes and cadmium (Cd2+) have been classified as toxic agents and act as a highly serious risk to affecting the human and environment (Elango et al., 2022a,b). In this work, we synthesized the CQDs from fish scale waste using a hydrothermal technique, and also its composition, functional group, morphology, and structural properties were then studied utilizing XRD, FTIR, TEM, and XPS analyses. Further, we also studied the photocatalytic properties of the CQDs to photodegrade methylene blue and reactive red 120 dye solution, as well as the metal ion detection and long-term stability.

Fish scales (L. rohita) were collected from the local market in Riyadh, Saudi Arabia. Copper chloride, ferrous sulfate, zinc acetate, cadmium chloride, cobalt chloride, mercuric chloride, silver nitrate and methylene blue and reactive red 120 dye were purchased from Sigma- Aldrich, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia. All the experiments were performed using double distilled water without further purification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CQDs from fish scales
ce:section.ce:para: The luminescence CQDs were synthesized from fish scales wastes by an easy one-step hydrothermal method. Fish scale wastes were collected from the traditional market in Riyadh Region, Saudi Arabia. In a conventional test, the raw fish scales were rinsed with DI water, dehydrated, and crushed the fine particles. Then, 5 gm of fish scales powder was added to the 100 mL of distilled water and continuously stirred for 1 h. Further, the mixture of samples was transferred in 200 mL of Teflon lined stainless steel autoclave and kept in a hot air oven at 160 °C for 18 h. After hydrothermal treatment, the obtained dark brown crude product was filtered and centrifuged at 12,000 rpm for 10 min (three times). The attained CQDs were kept at 4 °C in the refrigerator. The stored CQDs solutions were taken out and treated in the vacuum freeze-dried to get the powder form.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) is used to study the crystalline properties of the CQDs photocatalysts, Rigaku X-ray diffractometer, Japan, scanning window 2θ = 5°–70° (40 KV and 40 mA) analysis. An X-ray photoelectron spectroscope (XPS, Thermo Scientific XPS spectrometer), was used to determine the chemical compositions and element quantification of the CQDs. The size and structure were analyzed by transmission electron microscope (JEOL JEM-2010). UV–Visible spectroscopy (SHIMADZU-UV 2600 spectrometer, Japan) was applied to investigate the metal ions detection of the prepared luminance CQDs.

To investigate the detection of metal ions by fish scales' luminance CQDs, suitable dilutions of various metal ions such as Hg2+, Fe2+, Cu2+, Ni2+, and Cd2+ were used. In brief, aqueous solutions of (100 μM) concentration of each metal ion were diluted in a PBS solution to reduce the pH fluctuation caused by the hydrolysis of metal ions. About 2 mL solution of fish scale luminance CQDs (1 mg mL−1) was added to the 3 mL of metal ion solution and allowed for the reaction to take place at room temperature for 30 min. The absorption of CQDs with different metal ions was logged with a known concentration. The specific detection of Cd2+ ions (50–250 μM) using CQDs was established by the linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity and ion concentration.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic activity of methylene blue (MB) and reactive red 120 (RR120)
ce:section.ce:para:
The photocatalytic activity of Methylene blue (MB) and Reactive Red 120 (RR120) was investigated using a visible light resource. Initially, the reaction suspension was made by the addition of 50 mg CQDs into 45 mL of 40 μM MB and 20 ppm RR120 dye solution. The photocatalytic activity experiments were performed in Pyrex glass vessels at room temperature. Before irradiation, the MB and RR120 dye solution was agitated for 30 min in the dark to attain the adsorption-desorption equilibrium. To reduce heat during the photodegradation process, the photoreactor assembly integrates a well-organized water-circulating system. The reactor was exposed to a Xenon arc lamp that simulated solar light, equipped with a UV cut-off filter (λ ≥ 420 nm). During the irradiation, about 1.5 mL aliquots were withdrawn every 30 min from the reaction vessel by using a syringe filter system (pore size 0.2 nm). The concentration of filtrate dye solution was then evaluated by UV–vis spectrophotometry (Shimadzu UV-2600), where a wavelength (λ max) of 554 nm was used for the absorbance measurements of MB and RR120 dye. The earlier equation was used to calculate the degradation efficiency of freshly produced CQDs. The degradation efficacy (%) was defined as (1 − C
t
/C
0 ) × 100, where C
0 is the initial absorbance in solution, and C
t is the dye absorbance at reaction time (t).

XRD measurements were used to determine the crystalline phase of CQDs, and the associated XRD patterns are shown in Fig. 1 . The X-ray diffraction measurement shows a wide range of spectrum absorbed at 24 °C due to the fine dimension and controlled carbonization of the CQDs (Singhal et al., 2017). Additionally, the wide spectrum results were confirming the amorphous structure of the fish-scale waste-synthesized CQDs (Zhao et al., 2020). Recently, Elango et al. (2022) stated related XRD reports, which explain fluorescence CQDs synthesized from shrimp shell wastes by a simple bottom-up method. The bio-waste fabricated luminescence carbon quantum dots have great crystalline shapes (Elango et al., 2022a,b; Manikandan and Lee, 2022).

FT-IR analysis is employed for the characterization of both organic and inorganic functional groups present in fabricated CQDs materials. The FTIR analysis of fish-scale wasted fabricated luminescence carbon quantum dots is demonstrated in Fig. 2 . The FTIR spectrum results show a strong band located at 3411 and 2354 cm−1 corresponding to O–H and methyl (C–H) chemical groups due to the presence of the polyethylene functional groups (Das et al., 2019a; El Sharkawy et al., 2020). Also, the sharp peaks at 1618 and 1070 cm−1 match the chain of custody and carbonyl group stretch, respectively. The availability of C–O–C and C–H functional groups on the edge of the bio-fabricated luminescence CQDs is a particularly hydrophilic group (Wang et al., 2020). The small peak that appeared at 849 cm−1 matches the stretch type of CO, –NH, NH2, and C–O groups shown on the carbon quantum dots, correspondingly (Zhao et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2022). The FTIR reports are an important confirmatory test for the development of CQDs.

The structural morphology and particle size of the nanomaterials were analyzed through transmission electron microscope analysis. Fig. 3 (a–d) exhibited the morphology and size range of fish-scale wastes synthesized luminescence CQDs. The structure of the luminescence CQDs showed a smooth and spherical form and average particle size at around 10 nm (Chunduri et al., 2016; Mahesh et al., 2022). The HR-TEM pictures exhibit an equivalent distribution of carbon quantum dots (Fig. 3a–d). The TEM images recommend the amorphous crystal structure of the fish-scale fabricated CQDs (Abbas et al., 2020). Earlier, Ren et al., 2022 described that Agroforestry Waste derived carbon quantum dots got smooth and spherical structures and equal particle size, indicating the homogeneity of the crystalline and amorphous luminescence CQDs.

The chemical state and binding energy of the nanomaterials in the bio-fabricated luminescence CQDs sample have been explained by XPS analysis and the result is shown in Fig. 4 (a–d). Fig. 4a shows the survey spectrum that was seen as well as the high-resolution spectra of the C1s, N1s, and O1s for carbon quantum dots. Fig. 4b–d displays the XPS evaluation of C1s, N1s, and O1s of bio-fabricated carbon quantum dots. The N1s scales demonstrated a broad and strong band placed at 399.9 eV, exhibiting amine and heterocyclic groups (Fig. 5 b) (Lin et al., 2022). The allocation of the synthesized carbon dots element is noticed in Fig. 4c. The XPS results reveal that CQDs hydrocarbons (C–C/C–H) are responsible for the main peak at 283.6 eV, and also that the chemisorbed carbon species in the forms of C–O and CO are responsible for the binding energies of C1s at 285.2 eV and 286.8 eV, respectively (Wang et al., 2019). As seen in Fig. 4d, the O 1s have big and sharp peaks located at 532.2 eV are attributed to the lattice oxygen atoms and surface-adsorbed water, respectively (Xu et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2023a). Nevertheless, XPS measurement reports contained several oxygen and nitrogen chemical groups that can help with their excellent water solubility and high luminescence (Kalaiyarasan et al., 2020). The binding energy, chemical composition, and electronic state of atoms within the developed carbon dots can be interpreted by XPS.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic performance of MB and RR120
ce:section.ce:para: The photocatalytic degradation of methylene blue and reactive red 120 dyes (15 mg/L−1) and catalyst dosage 20 mg/L were used to test the photocatalytic performance of as-fabricated luminescence CQDs. Fig. 5a and b shows the standardized changes in the methylene blue and reactive red 120 dyes aqueous solution concentration photocatalyzed by blank, CQDs-dark, and CQDs-visible light irradiation (C/C0) (Li et al., 2022). A comparison of the degradation rates of methylene blue and reactive red 120 dyes by blank, CQDs-dark, and CQDs-visible light is shown in Fig. 5a and b. The initial blank response, which occurs in the presence of VL irradiation but without CQDs, results in approximately 1.4% and 1.6% degradation of both dyes, respectively (Wang et al., 2023b). According to the data shown in Fig. 5c, the degradation rate of blank, CQDs-dark, and CQDs-visible light was found to be 1.4, 26, and 96.5% when used to remove methylene blue, while the values were 1.6, 26.2, and 97.8% when reactive red 120 dyes. The main reason why CQDs-visible light outperforms blank, CQDs-dark in terms of photocatalytic performance for methylene blue and reactive red 120 dyes is that the photogenerated electrons from CQDs excited by a light source are trapped by the CQDs edge, preventing electron-hole pair recombination (Usman et al., 2019; Bordbar et al., 2017). Fig. 5d illustrates the photocatalytic degradation process by which as-fabricated CQDs degraded methylene blue and reactive red 120 dyes. Corresponding to the degradation qualities, inducing the visible light has been a successful way to increase the methylene blue and reactive red 120 dyes degradation of bio-fabricated CQDs. When exposed to visible light (420 nm), electrons in CQDs VB are encouraged to move toward the CB (Vinoth Kumar et al., 2021). The O2 radicals cannot be generated because the CB of CQDs is situated at an energy greater than the O2/⋅O2− conversion potential (Abd Rani et al., 2022; You et al., 2020). The energy of the VB of CQDs is higher than the potential for OH−/⋅OH conversion, ⋅OH radicals can be produced. The increased photocatalytic efficiency of the material might be associated with the absorption of organic molecules on the photocatalyst surface, the catalyst's adsorptive ability, and the inhibition of photogenerated electrons pair replication. As shown in Fig. 5d, increasing the quantity of CQDs enhanced the average photodegradation of MB and RR120. The photocatalytic activities were suppressed when the number of CQDs continued to increase further (Zhao et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2019). In conclusion, the enhanced visible light absorption, improved charge separation, effective electron transfer, produced hydroxyl radicals, the improved absorptivity of methylene blue and reactive red 120 dyes, and large surface contact between CQDS-dark is thought to be the causes of the high-efficiency photocatalytic activity of the luminescence CQDs (Molaei, 2019; Guo et al., 2020).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Kinetics study of MB and RR120
ce:section.ce:para: Using the kinetics first-order fitting, the noticeable degradation rate constants of the blank, CQDs-Dark, and CQDs-visible light as prepared toward the degradation of methylene blue and reactive Red 120 dyes were calculated [Govarthanan et al., 2022; Elumalai et al., 2021]. When C0 represents the initial absorbance, Ct represents the absorbance at time t, and k represents the first-order rate constant, the rate equation is given as ln (C0/Ct) = kt. Fig. 6 (a and b) exhibits a plot between ln (C0/C) and response time (Palanisamy et al., 2022a,b). According to degradation experiments using blank, CQDs-Dark, and CQDs-visible lights as photocatalysts for the removal of methylene blue and reactive red 120 dyes, the apparent rate constants (kapp) were determined to be 0.899, 0.954, and 0.983 min−1 for methylene blue dye degradation, respectively. Similar results were achieved for the degradation of reactive red 120 dye, with values of 0.972, 0.986, and 0.989 min−1, respectively (Fig. 6b).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability study of MB and RR120
ce:section.ce:para: The performances of photocatalytic degradation of the methylene blue and reactive red 120 dyes for luminescence CQDs under visible light irradiation over three successive runs were investigated to determine the photo-stability of the CQDs, as shown in Fig. 7 a and b. We duplicated the same experimental methods for our photocatalytic degradation testing. After three photodegradation tests, the bio-fabrication CQDs nanomaterials still show greater than 70% degradation of both dyes. These imply that the fish-scale waste synthesized CQDs photocatalyst exhibits satisfactory stability during photocatalytic activity. The loss of photocatalysts during sample collection by centrifugation and drying for reuse in the photocatalytic experiment may be the cause of the observed drop in the photodegradation rate (Dager et al., 2019; Priyadharshini et al., 2021). The bio-waste fabricated CQDs strong photocatalytic activity and stability are especially advantageous to their practical use.

To decide the selective and sensitive detection of metal ions by the bio-waste fabricated luminances CQDs, various metal ions (Hg2+, Fe2+, Cu2+, Ni2+, and Cd2+) were used (Fig. 8 a). By way of analyzing the diagram, we noticed that Cd2+ metal ions (Fig. 8b) demonstrated effective exposure with excessive (F0–F/F0). The results believe the linear relationship among the comparative intensity of luminescence CQDs with different metal ions. The possibility of the bio-waste-prepared CQDs to detect Cd2+ was revealed in terms of a decrease in fluorescence intensity (quantum quenching) of CQDs with Cd2+ concentrations (50–250 μM). The distinctive reaction of the synthesized CQDs indicates linear relation with the concentrations of cadmium ions as shown in Fig. (Fig. 8c and d). Henceforth, it can be found that, compared with other metal ions, only Cd2+ had a significant quenching effect on the fluorescence intensity of CQDs, whereas other metal ions had a smaller effect (Boruah et al., 2020; Das et al., 2019b). The PL intensity also tends to reduce when a higher concentration of Cd2+ ions is mixed with fabricated CQDs solutions. This condition infers the quenching phenomenon that occurred in the CQDs structure upon the accumulation of heavy metal ions (Šafranko et al., 2021). Furthermore, Cd2+ metal ions get an h+ from the ligand, hence reducing it to cadmium (Cd2+) alone. Hence, this outcome of the results suggests that the bio-waste fabricated luminescence CQDs have a superior capability for the detection of Cd2+ in an aqueous ecosystem. Therefore, this is a favorable method for the detection of heavy metal ions in polluted water and manufacturing overflows.

In summary, we have synthesized novel high-quality luminescence carbon quantum dots use of fish scale waste as a source. The fabricated CQDs showed efficient photocatalytic degradation performance of methylene blue and reactive red 120 dyes under visible light (420 nm) irradiation. The photocatalytic degradation performance under visible light irradiation might be related to the considerably larger visual absorption of CQDs. The photocatalytic degradation efficiencies of methylene blue and reactive red 120 dyes were 96.5 and 97 8%, respectively, after 120 min of exposure. Notably, the collaborative combination of CQDs and visible lights may provide high photocatalytic activity in the oxidation of organic dye solutions. After three cycles of recycled CQDs, the photocatalytic degradation of methylene blue and reactive red 120 dyes was slightly hampered. Additionally, the fish scale waste synthesized CQDs have outstanding luminesce stability and were employed for the detection of cadmium ions in an aqueous solution. This work offers a promising photocatalytic material for methylene blue and reactive red 120 dye degradation and detection of cadmium ions in aqueous ecosystem.

Ghedeir M. Alshammari: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Writing- Original draft preparation, Supervision. Mohammed S. Al-Ayed: Data curation, Writing- Original draft preparation. Mohamed Anwar Abdelhalim: Visualization, Data curation, Investigation. Laila Naif Al-Harbi: Software, Validation, Data curation. Akram A. Qasem: Conceptualization, Methodology. Mohammed Abdo Yahya: Writing- Reviewing and Editing. Name: Visualization, Investigation.

This project was funded by the National Plan for Science, Technology, and Innovation (MAARIFAH), King Abdulaziz City for Science and Technology, Kingdom of Saudi Arabia, Award Number (3-17-10-001-0003).

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors extend thanks to the National Plan for Science, Technology, and Innovation (MAARIFAH), King Abdulaziz City for Science and Technology, Kingdom of Saudi Arabia, Award Number (3-17-10-001-0003) for funding this work.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Castañeda-Serna et al., 2022
